title: One thing I love about...The Walking Dead: Here's Negan: The Board Game
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=s2GjWfPbh9I

Daniel (@dungeondive) admits to opening the video with characteristic understatement that he probably talks about **Here's Negan** more than every other board-game YouTube channel combined. The game is, on his reading, one of the most underrated co-op board games in the hobby — *the* only game he knows that gives him the tension of Space Hulk while being built for co-op and solo from the ground up rather than retrofitted to it.

The thing he loves: **Negan himself**, as an AI-controlled NPC. No player runs him. The board does. At the end of every turn, an action card is drawn from the Negan deck and resolved, and the deck does the work of *embodying* him — petulant, capricious, occasionally helpful, mostly not. He might refuse to move because he's *not in the mood*. He might dock a nearby survivor's reputation for *being scared*. He might pluck the lowest-reputation survivor and drag them three squares toward the nearest walker. Reputation, in a quietly clever twist on co-op, is *also* the implicit competitive layer: whichever survivor finishes the mission with the highest standing in Negan's gang wins the session.

Daniel singles out Mantic's comic-book-sized rule book as a model of how to size a rule book — and the game's compact medium-small box as a model of how to right-size component density without skimping on variety.

----

*Here's Negan* layers a hidden reputation race onto a co-op chassis — co-op against the zombies, secretly competitive against your teammates for Negan's favour. Is that a tension you'd want in more co-ops, or does it ruin the table feel?
